prompt: What is the defining difference between streaming and classic request-response?
choices: Streaming compresses the response so it uses less bandwidth
Streaming sends the answer incrementally as it is produced, instead of all at once at the end
Streaming always finishes faster in total elapsed time
Streaming removes the need for the client to make any request

prompt: Which situations are good fits for streaming or real-time push? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A long, gradually generated text answer where the user should see progress immediately
Live prices or scores that change continuously and should appear the moment they update
A tiny one-shot lookup that returns a single value in a few milliseconds
A chat feed where new messages should arrive without the client repeatedly asking

prompt: Why can streaming dramatically improve the *perceived* performance of a slow response even when total time is unchanged?
choices: Because it reduces the total number of bytes sent
Because time-to-first-byte drops, so the user sees output almost immediately instead of a frozen wait
Because it moves all computation onto the client
Because open connections are always faster than closed ones

prompt: Which of these are real costs or risks introduced by streaming and real-time push that plain request-response avoids? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Open connections consume server resources for their whole lifetime
A connection can drop mid-stream, requiring reconnection or resume logic
A fast producer can overwhelm a slow consumer (back-pressure)
The response can no longer contain any text
